section.title: The Fall of Samaria
section.sectionRationale: After Hoshea's failed rebellion, Assyria conquers Samaria and exiles the northern kingdom permanently. The chapter pauses the narrative to give the author's own extended theological verdict on why Israel fell, tracing two and a half centuries of persistent idolatry back to the golden calves, then describes the mixed, syncretistic worship of the foreign peoples resettled in Israel's place.
section.thesis: The fall of the northern kingdom was not an accident of geopolitics but the LORD's just and long-warned judgment on centuries of covenant unfaithfulness, a verdict so decisive that even the pagan settlers who replace Israel inherit a warning about worshiping the LORD alongside other gods.
section.frameNote: Four beats: Hoshea's reign and Samaria's fall, verses 1 to 6; the author's extended theological explanation of Israel's sin, verses 7 to 23; the resettlement of Samaria by foreign peoples, verses 24 to 34; and the LORD's covenant demand for exclusive worship, verses 35 to 41.

units.label: Hoshea's reign and Samaria's fall
units.note: the king of Assyria captured Samaria

verses.text: In the twelfth year of the reign of Ahaz over Judah, Hoshea son of Elah became king of Israel, and he reigned in Samaria nine years.
And he did evil in the sight of the LORD, but not like the kings of Israel who preceded him.
Shalmaneser king of Assyria attacked him, and Hoshea became his vassal and paid him tribute.

But the king of Assyria discovered that Hoshea had conspired to send envoys to King So of Egypt, and that he had not paid tribute to the king of Assyria as in previous years. Therefore the king of Assyria arrested Hoshea and put him in prison.
Then the king of Assyria invaded the whole land, marched up to Samaria, and besieged it for three years.

In the ninth year of Hoshea, the king of Assyria captured Samaria and carried away the Israelites to Assyria, where he settled them in Halah, in Gozan by the Habor River, and in the cities of the Medes.

All this happened because the people of Israel had sinned against the LORD their God, who had brought them out of the land of Egypt from under the hand of Pharaoh king of Egypt. They had worshiped other gods
and walked in the customs of the nations that the LORD had driven out before the Israelites, as well as in the practices introduced by the kings of Israel.
The Israelites secretly did things against the LORD their God that were not right. From watchtower to fortified city, they built high places in all their cities.
They set up for themselves sacred pillars and Asherah poles on every high hill and under every green tree.
They burned incense on all the high places like the nations that the LORD had driven out before them. They did wicked things, provoking the LORD to anger.
They served {{pesel|idols}}, although the LORD had told them, "You shall not do this thing."

Yet through all His prophets and seers, the LORD warned Israel and Judah, saying, "Turn from your wicked ways and keep My commandments and statutes, according to the entire Law that I commanded your fathers and delivered to you through My servants the prophets."
But they would not listen, and they stiffened their {{oref|necks}} like their fathers, who did not believe the LORD their God.

They rejected His statutes and the covenant He had made with their fathers, as well as the decrees He had given them. They pursued worthless idols and became worthless themselves, going after the surrounding nations that the LORD had commanded them not to imitate.
They abandoned all the commandments of the LORD their God and made for themselves two cast idols of calves and an Asherah pole. They bowed down to all the host of heaven and served Baal.
They sacrificed their sons and daughters in the fire and practiced divination and soothsaying. They devoted themselves to doing evil in the sight of the LORD, provoking Him to anger.
So the LORD was very angry with Israel, and He removed them from His presence. Only the tribe of Judah remained,
and even Judah did not keep the commandments of the LORD their God, but lived according to the customs Israel had introduced.
So the LORD rejected all the descendants of Israel. He afflicted them and delivered them into the hands of plunderers, until He had banished them from His presence.
When the LORD had torn Israel away from the house of David, they made Jeroboam son of Nebat king, and Jeroboam led Israel away from following the LORD and caused them to commit a great sin.
The Israelites persisted in all the sins that Jeroboam had committed and did not turn away from them.

Finally, the LORD removed Israel from His presence, as He had declared through all His servants the prophets. So Israel was {{galah|exiled}} from their homeland into Assyria, where they are to this day.

units.label: Foreign peoples resettle Samaria
units.note: the LORD sent lions among them

verses.text: Then the king of Assyria brought people from Babylon, Cuthah, Avva, Hamath, and Sepharvaim and settled them in the towns of Samaria to replace the Israelites. They took possession of Samaria and lived in its towns.
Now when the settlers first lived there, they did not worship the LORD, so He sent lions among them, which killed some of them.

So they spoke to the king of Assyria, saying, "The peoples that you have removed and placed in the cities of Samaria do not know the requirements of the God of the land. Because of this, He has sent lions among them, which are indeed killing them off."
Then the king of Assyria commanded: "Send back one of the priests you carried off from Samaria, and have him go back to live there and teach the requirements of the God of the land."
Thus one of the priests they had carried away came and lived in Bethel, and he began to teach them how they should worship the LORD.

Nevertheless, the people of each nation continued to make their own gods in the cities where they had settled, and they set them up in the shrines that the people of Samaria had made on the high places.
The men of Babylon made Succoth-benoth, the men of Cuth made Nergal, the men of Hamath made Ashima,
the Avvites made Nibhaz and Tartak, and the Sepharvites burned their children in the fire to Adrammelech and Anammelech the gods of Sepharvaim.
So the new residents worshiped the LORD, but they also appointed for themselves priests of all sorts to serve in the shrines of the high places.
They worshiped the LORD, but they also served their own gods according to the customs of the nations from which they had been carried away.

To this day they are still practicing their former customs. None of them worship the LORD or observe the statutes, ordinances, laws, and commandments that the LORD gave the descendants of Jacob, whom He named Israel.

units.label: The covenant demand for exclusive worship

verses.text: For the LORD had made a covenant with the Israelites and commanded them, "Do not {{yare|worship}} other gods or bow down to them; do not serve them or sacrifice to them.
Instead, worship the LORD, who brought you out of the land of Egypt with great power and an outstretched arm. You are to bow down to Him and offer sacrifices to Him.
And you must always be careful to observe the statutes, ordinances, laws, and commandments He wrote for you. Do not worship other gods.
Do not forget the covenant I have made with you. Do not worship other gods,
but worship the LORD your God, and He will deliver you from the hands of all your enemies."
But they would not listen, and they persisted in their former customs.
So these nations worshiped the LORD but also served their idols, and to this day their children and grandchildren continue to do as their fathers did.

pesel.gloss: A carved or cast idol, explicitly forbidden by the second commandment, Exodus 20:4. That Israel served pesel after the LORD had specifically said, you shall not do this thing, underscores that their judgment came not from ignorance of God's will but from willful, repeated defiance of a command they knew perfectly well.

oref.gloss: Neck, used idiomatically for stubborn resistance, to stiffen the oref meaning to refuse correction like an animal that will not be led. The same phrase describes Israel at Sinai, Exodus 32:9, showing this final judgment answers a pattern of resistance that had characterized the nation from its very beginning.

galah.gloss: To uncover, remove, or go into exile. This is the pivotal word of the entire book, marking the fulfillment of the covenant curse threatened in Leviticus 26 and Deuteronomy 28, and setting the trajectory that Judah itself will follow in 2 Kings 25 when it, too, is finally exiled from the land.

yare.gloss: To fear, revere, or worship, though the BSB here renders it worship in the specific sense of reverent submission owed exclusively to the LORD. The covenant repeatedly commands Israel not to yare other gods but to reserve this reverence entirely for the LORD who redeemed them from Egypt, the very command generations of kings and people alike refused to keep.

basic.context: Hoshea, Israel's last king, rebels against Assyria by seeking Egyptian help and withholding tribute, prompting a three-year siege that ends with Samaria's capture and the permanent exile of the northern kingdom's population. The author pauses the narrative here to deliver an extended theological verdict, tracing Israel's fall not to bad luck or weak politics but to over two centuries of persistent idolatry, beginning with Jeroboam's golden calves and continuing despite repeated prophetic warnings, until the LORD finally removed Israel from His presence exactly as He had declared. The chapter closes by describing the mixed, syncretistic worship of the foreign peoples Assyria resettled in Israel's place, who worshiped the LORD alongside their own gods, and by restating the covenant's original demand for Israel's exclusive worship of the LORD alone.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative combined with the author's own inspired theological commentary explaining the meaning of the events just narrated; it functions as the interpretive key for the entire book of Kings. What endures is the sober truth that persistent, unrepented covenant unfaithfulness brings real historical consequences, and that partial or mixed worship, giving the LORD some allegiance alongside other loyalties, ultimately fails to honor Him as He requires.
basic.originalLanguages: Israel served pesel, carved idols, in direct defiance of a command they clearly knew, and stiffened their oref, neck, refusing correction exactly as their ancestors had at Sinai. The result is galah, exile, the pivotal covenant curse now fully realized and destined to fall on Judah as well in the book's final chapters. And the covenant's repeated demand that Israel yare, worship or revere, the LORD exclusively, rather than alongside other gods, frames the entire chapter's verdict on both Israel's fall and the resettled peoples' incomplete devotion.
basic.typology: Israel's persistent, willful covenant unfaithfulness culminating in exile previews humanity's own persistent rebellion against God despite repeated warning, while the demand for wholehearted, exclusive worship anticipates the New Testament's insistence that no one can serve two masters, a truth fulfilled perfectly only in Christ's undivided devotion to the Father.
basic.christ: The prophets sent again and again to call wayward Israel back before judgment fell anticipates the ultimate Prophet, Jesus Christ, who likewise came to His own people with a final and greater call to repentance, Matthew 23:37-38, and whose rejection, like the prophets before Him, still did not exhaust God's patience but led instead to the offer of salvation extended to all nations, exactly as the exile scattered Israel yet ultimately served God's larger redemptive purposes.
basic.god: God is patient, sending prophet after prophet across centuries before finally executing the judgment He had long warned of, showing that His judgment, however severe, is neither impulsive nor unjust but the fitting response to sustained, willful rebellion. He is also exclusively jealous, refusing to share worship with any other god, a demand rooted not in pettiness but in His unique identity as the one true God who alone redeemed Israel from Egypt.
basic.crossReferences: Leviticus 26:27-33 and Deuteronomy 28:36-37, 64 foretold precisely this exile centuries in advance, showing the LORD's word, not mere political circumstance, governed Israel's fall. Exodus 32:9 first describes Israel as stiff-necked at Sinai, the same charge repeated here at the end of the northern kingdom's history. Matthew 6:24 teaches that no one can serve two masters, directly addressing the mixed worship the resettled peoples practice at this chapter's close.
basic.oneStory: This chapter is the theological hinge of the entire book of Kings, explaining in the author's own voice why Israel fell: not political misfortune but centuries of willful covenant unfaithfulness met, at last, by God's long-threatened and perfectly just judgment, a pattern that will soon overtake Judah as well and that finds its ultimate resolution only in Christ, whose perfect, undivided devotion to the Father succeeds where Israel and every mixed-worship nation after it had failed.

context.literary: This chapter functions as a deliberate narrative pause, breaking from the ongoing king-by-king chronicle to deliver an extended, first-person theological explanation, the longest such editorial reflection in the entire book, before resuming historical narrative with the reign of Hezekiah in chapter 18.
context.historical: Assyrian records independently confirm the fall of Samaria under Sargon II, who completed the siege begun by Shalmaneser V, and record the deportation of Israelite populations and resettlement of foreign peoples in their place, a standard Assyrian imperial policy designed to prevent future rebellion by dispersing conquered populations and mixing new settlers among them. The resulting mixed-worship population described here forms the ethnic and religious background of the later Samaritans encountered in the New Testament.
context.canonical: This chapter's theological verdict on Israel directly anticipates the fall of Judah narrated in chapters 24 to 25, using nearly identical covenant-curse language, and its warning against divided worship, serving the LORD alongside other gods, echoes throughout the prophets and finds its clearest New Testament statement in Jesus' teaching that no one can serve two masters, Matthew 6:24.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To provide the theological explanation for Israel's fall as an act of God's just, patient, but ultimately decisive judgment on centuries of covenant unfaithfulness, warning readers, including Judah, that persistent idolatry and divided worship cannot escape the same consequences.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter is unusually explicit theological commentary rather than mere narrative description, directly interpreting the meaning of the events for its readers; its warning against divided or mixed worship remains a directly applicable, enduring principle rather than a culturally bound description alone.

debates.question: How should the mixed worship of the resettled peoples, verses 32 to 41, be assessed, given that they did, in some sense, worship the LORD?

views.label: Genuinely condemned as false worship despite including the LORD
views.case: The passage repeatedly and pointedly states they worshiped the LORD but also served their idols, and explicitly concludes none of them worship the LORD in the full covenantal sense, verse 34, showing that including the LORD alongside other gods does not constitute acceptable worship at all; the LORD's exclusive claim, restated at length in verses 35 to 39, cannot be partially honored.

views.label: A transitional, partially instructed devotion later refined
views.case: The narrative notes a priest was specifically sent to teach them the requirements of the God of the land, verse 28, suggesting an initial stage of incomplete but genuinely developing knowledge of the LORD among a population new to the land, whose descendants, the later Samaritans, retained some meaningful, if theologically incomplete, connection to worship of Israel's God into the New Testament period, John 4:20-22.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the text explicitly condemns divided worship as failing to meet the LORD's covenant demand for exclusive devotion, while differing on how much genuine, if incomplete, knowledge of the LORD may have developed among these communities over subsequent generations.

study.originalLanguages: Israel served pesel, carved idols, in direct, willful defiance of a command they knew clearly, and stiffened their oref, neck, refusing correction just as their ancestors had at Sinai, Exodus 32:9. The result is galah, exile, the pivotal covenant curse of Leviticus 26 and Deuteronomy 28 now fully and finally realized, a fate the book's closing chapters show will soon overtake Judah as well. And the covenant's repeated, insistent demand that Israel yare, revere or worship, the LORD exclusively rather than alongside any other god frames the chapter's entire verdict, both on Israel's fall and on the incomplete devotion of the peoples who replace them.

typology.type: Centuries of persistent covenant unfaithfulness met at last by patient but decisive judgment
typology.fulfillment: The pattern of God's forbearance toward humanity's rebellion, ultimately answered in the cross where judgment and mercy meet, Romans 3:25-26.

typology.type: Worship divided between the LORD and other gods, ultimately condemned as no true worship at all
typology.fulfillment: Christ's teaching that no one can serve two masters, requiring undivided devotion to God alone, Matthew 6:24.

study.christ: The prophets sent generation after generation to call wayward Israel back from judgment, only to be repeatedly refused, anticipates the ultimate Prophet, Jesus Christ, who came to His own people with a final and greater call to repentance and was likewise rejected, Matthew 23:37-38, yet whose rejection did not exhaust God's redemptive purposes but instead opened salvation to all nations, just as Israel's exile, however tragic, ultimately served God's larger unfolding plan. The mixed worship condemned at this chapter's close, serving the LORD alongside other gods, anticipates Christ's own uncompromising teaching that no one can serve two masters, Matthew 6:24, and only His perfect, undivided devotion to the Father fully satisfies the exclusive worship God has always required.
study.god: God is remarkably patient, sending prophet after prophet across more than two centuries before finally executing the judgment He had long warned of, proving that His judgment, however severe, is neither impulsive nor unjust but the fitting and fully deserved response to sustained, willful, repeatedly warned rebellion. He is also exclusively jealous for His people's worship, refusing to accept divided devotion that includes Him alongside other gods, a demand rooted in His unique identity as the one true God who alone redeemed Israel from Egypt and alone is worthy of complete trust and worship.

crossReferences.note: Foretells centuries in advance the exact exile and scattering that befalls Israel in this chapter, showing God's word, not mere circumstance, governed the outcome.
Further specifies the covenant curse of exile among foreign nations, precisely fulfilled in Israel's deportation to Assyria.
First describes Israel as stiff-necked at Sinai, the same charge repeated here at the tragic conclusion of the northern kingdom's history.
Christ's teaching that no one can serve two masters directly addresses the mixed, divided worship condemned throughout the closing verses of this chapter.

study.oneStory: This chapter stands as the theological hinge of the entire book of Kings, explaining in the author's own inspired voice why the northern kingdom fell: not misfortune or weak politics, but centuries of willful, repeatedly warned covenant unfaithfulness met at last by God's perfectly just and long-patient judgment. This same pattern will soon overtake Judah as well, and it finds its ultimate resolution only in Jesus Christ, whose complete, undivided devotion to the Father succeeds where Israel, and every nation practicing divided worship after it, had utterly failed.

quiz.q: What led directly to the king of Assyria's final siege and capture of Samaria?

options.text: Hoshea conspired with Egypt's king and stopped paying tribute to Assyria, prompting Assyria's king to imprison him and besiege the city for three years.
options.note: Verses 3 to 6 record this exact sequence of rebellion and its consequences.

options.text: Israel launched an unprovoked invasion of Assyrian territory.
options.note: The text describes Hoshea withholding tribute and seeking Egyptian alliance, not an Israelite invasion of Assyria.

options.text: Samaria surrendered voluntarily without any siege.
options.note: The text explicitly describes a three-year siege before the city's capture.

options.text: Assyria attacked without any prior provocation from Israel.
options.note: The text specifically attributes the invasion to Hoshea's rebellion and withheld tribute.

quiz.q: According to the author's theological explanation, what was the root cause of Israel's fall?

options.text: Centuries of persistent idolatry and covenant unfaithfulness, despite repeated warnings from the LORD's prophets, going back to the sins Jeroboam introduced.
options.note: Verses 7 to 23 give this extended, explicit explanation as the direct cause of the exile.

options.text: Simply poor military strategy and weak alliances.
options.note: The author's stated explanation is thoroughly theological and covenantal, not merely a commentary on military or political strategy.

options.text: The size and strength of the Assyrian empire alone.
options.note: While Assyria's power is the historical instrument, the author explicitly attributes the ultimate cause to Israel's own persistent sin.

options.text: The text gives no explanation for Israel's fall.
options.note: Verses 7 to 23 constitute one of the longest theological explanations in the entire book.

quiz.q: Why did the LORD send lions among the foreign peoples resettled in Samaria?

options.text: Because the new settlers did not worship the LORD, the God of the land they now inhabited.
options.note: Verse 25 gives this exact and explicit reason.

options.text: Because they had destroyed all the remaining Israelite shrines.
options.note: The text attributes the lions specifically to their failure to worship the LORD, not to destroying shrines.

options.text: Because they refused to pay tribute to the king of Assyria.
options.note: The lions are explicitly tied to religious failure to worship the LORD, not political tribute issues.

options.text: The lions are never explained in the text.
options.note: Verse 25 provides a clear, explicit explanation for this event.

quiz.q: How is the worship of the resettled peoples ultimately described at the end of the chapter?

options.text: They worshiped the LORD but also served their own idols according to their former customs, a divided worship the text explicitly says fails to count as truly worshiping the LORD.
options.note: Verses 33 to 34 and 41 record this exact, pointed description of mixed, divided worship.

options.text: They fully abandoned all their former gods and worshiped only the LORD.
options.note: The text explicitly states they continued serving their own gods alongside the LORD, not full abandonment of idolatry.

options.text: They rejected the LORD entirely and worshiped only their native gods.
options.note: The text specifically notes they did include some worship of the LORD, though incompletely and alongside idols.

options.text: No description of their worship practices is given.
options.note: Verses 24 to 41 give an extended and detailed description of their mixed worship practices.

quiz.q: What did the LORD's covenant specifically require of Israel regarding other gods, as restated in verses 35 to 39?

options.text: To worship the LORD exclusively, never bowing down to, serving, or sacrificing to any other god, since He alone had brought them out of Egypt.
options.note: Verses 35 to 39 restate this exact exclusive covenant demand in detail.

options.text: To worship the LORD as the primary god while permitting minor local deities.
options.note: The text explicitly and repeatedly forbids worship of any other god whatsoever, allowing no exceptions.

options.text: To worship whichever god seemed most powerful at any given time.
options.note: The covenant demand is fixed and exclusive to the LORD alone, not contingent on perceived power of rival gods.

options.text: The covenant made no specific demands regarding other gods.
options.note: Verses 35 to 39 give an extended, explicit, and repeated demand for exclusive worship of the LORD.

questions: The LORD sent prophet after prophet for over two centuries before judgment finally fell on Israel. Where has God's patience with a persistent pattern in your own life been far longer than you perhaps deserved, and how should that shape your response now?

Israel is repeatedly described as stiffening its neck, refusing correction it clearly understood. Where might you be resisting a correction from God that you know full well but have simply refused to heed?

The resettled peoples worshiped the LORD alongside their own gods, and the text explicitly says this did not count as true worship at all. Where in your own life might you be trying to include God alongside other ultimate loyalties rather than giving Him your undivided devotion?

This chapter functions as the author's own explanation for why history unfolded as it did, rather than leaving events to speak for themselves. How comfortable are you interpreting difficult seasons in your own life through the lens of Scripture's explanations rather than assuming they are random or meaningless?

Israel's fall traces back to one early compromise, Jeroboam's golden calves, that was never fully corrected for generations. What early compromise in your own life or family history might still be quietly shaping choices today if left unaddressed?

translationNotes.note: King So of Egypt is likely an abbreviation for a specific Egyptian ruler, possibly Osorkon, though the exact historical identification remains debated among scholars.

The specific locations of exile, Halah, Gozan by the Habor River, and the cities of the Medes, are independently attested regions within the Assyrian empire, corroborating the historical reliability of this account.

The mixed population resettled in Samaria, combined with the remaining Israelites and the returning priest's incomplete instruction, forms the historical and religious background for the later Samaritans of the New Testament, whose worship the Gospel of John describes as incomplete, John 4:20-22.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Kings 17:1-41. No em dashes or en dashes appear in the source text for this chapter.
